conversation.question: My main problem right now taht Gotify notifications does not work on ios. And that the user experience becomes clunky eg finish book, wait for notification review book get suggestions. If we had an app we could make that very smooth <!----><!---->
conversation.answer:
You are hitting a very common and frustrating wall. It is completely understandable why that flow feels clunky—relying on an asynchronous push notification to drive a core user journey (Finish Book → Review → Get Suggestions) is a recipe for high drop-off rates, especially on Apple devices.

Here is the reality of your current setup, why a dedicated app fixes it, and a few bridge solutions you could use in the meantime.

The Gotify iOS Struggle is Real

Gotify is fantastic for self-hosting, but it natively struggles on iOS because of Apple's strict background processing rules. Apple requires push notifications to route through their own Apple Push Notification service (APNs). Because Gotify relies on persistent background WebSockets to deliver messages, iOS aggressively kills that connection to save battery.

There are third-party workarounds (like the community-built iGotify app), but they require extra helper containers and are infamous for dropping background connections anyway.

Why a Dedicated App is the Perfect Fix

You are spot on: a dedicated app would make this incredibly smooth. By moving this workflow into a native app, you change the entire paradigm from an asynchronous "wait and see" to a synchronous, instant flow.

If you had an app, the UX would look like this:

  • Instant Transitions: The user taps "Finish Book." Instead of waiting for a server to ping their phone, the app immediately routes them to the "Review Book" screen.

  • Background Fetching: While the user is typing their review, the app can make an API call in the background to generate or fetch their new book suggestions.

  • Seamless Delivery: The moment they hit "Submit Review," the suggestions are already loaded and waiting for them on the next screen. No push notifications required.

Bridge Solutions (If you aren't ready to build an app yet)

If building a full iOS app from scratch (via Swift, React Native, or Flutter) is too heavy of a lift right now, you have a couple of alternatives to smooth out the UX:

  • Build a Progressive Web App (PWA): If your service is web-based, you can optimize it into a PWA. Users can save it to their iOS home screen, giving it an app-like feel. Furthermore, iOS Safari now officially supports Web Push notifications, which are far more reliable than Gotify's background websockets.

  • Switch to NTFY.sh or Pushover: If you strictly want to keep the notification-based flow for now, swap Gotify for ntfy (which has a dedicated iOS app that handles backgrounding much better) or Pushover (a paid but bulletproof push service that routes correctly through APNs).

Are you leaning towards building a full mobile app right away, or would a Progressive Web App be a better stepping stone for your current tech stack?

conversation.question: I think building an app wil be the best end result. How often is the abs app updated? How hard will it be to keep up? <!----><!---->
conversation.answer:
Building a dedicated app (or front-end) to interface with Audiobookshelf (ABS) is definitely the ultimate fix for that flow. But you are asking the exact right question: maintaining a custom app alongside a rapidly moving open-source project can be tricky.

Here is a breakdown of how active the ABS ecosystem is and what you can expect if you decide to build alongside it.

How Often is Audiobookshelf Updated?

Audiobookshelf is an incredibly active project.

  • The Server: Updates frequently. Minor version bumps and bug fixes are pushed almost monthly (they just hit the v2.33.x releases in early 2026).

  • The Official App: Also very active. The official client is currently sitting at v0.12.x-beta and sees regular commits to fix bugs, add features, and keep parity with the server. It is built using NuxtJS and Capacitor (essentially a Vue web app wrapped for native devices).

How Hard Will it be to Keep Up?

The difficulty of keeping up depends entirely on how you choose to build your app.

1. Forking the Official App (Hard) If your plan is to fork the official advplyr/audiobookshelf-app GitHub repo and hack your custom "Review & Suggestions" flow directly into their codebase, keeping up will be a headache. Because the official app is updated so frequently, pulling in their upstream changes to your fork will result in constant merge conflicts.

2. Building a Custom Client/Companion App (Much Easier) If you build your own custom app from scratch—either a full audiobook player or just a lightweight companion app—keeping up is actually very manageable.

  • API Stability: The ABS REST API is fantastic, highly stable, and exceptionally well-documented (api.audiobookshelf.org). Server updates rarely break existing core API endpoints.

  • API Keys: In a recent major update (v2.26.0), ABS introduced dedicated, persistent API keys specifically designed for server-to-server communication, scripts, and external apps. This makes authenticating your custom app much easier than the old token system.

A Potential "Third Way"

Instead of building a full-blown audiobook player to replace the ABS app, you could build a lightweight Companion PWA (Progressive Web App). Users could listen to the book on the official ABS app or third-party iOS clients like ShelfPlayer or Plappa. When they finish a book, a webhook from ABS pings your backend, which then pushes a Web Push Notification (or a simpler native push) to your Companion App. When they tap it, it opens your custom UI for the review and suggestion engine, keeping the heavy lifting of audio playback entirely on the official tools.

Are you envisioning building a full replacement app that handles the actual audiobook listening and the suggestion flow, or just a lightweight companion app that handles the post-book experience?

conversation.question: Pwa would be an easier way. How do we handle notifications and starting playing a book from apwa? <!----><!---->
conversation.answer:
A Progressive Web App is a great middle-ground. It saves you from the nightmare of App Store approvals and native codebases, while still giving you that smooth, app-like feel.

Handling notifications and playback via a PWA on iOS used to be impossible, but Apple has recently opened the doors—with a few strict rules. Here is exactly how you handle both.

1. Handling PWA Notifications on iOS

Apple finally enabled Web Push notifications starting in iOS 16.4 (released in early 2023). However, unlike Android, Apple forces you through a very specific user journey to make it work.

Here are the rules of the game for iOS Web Push:

  • It Must Be "Installed": Push notifications will not work if the user is just looking at your site in Safari. They absolutely must tap Share → Add to Home Screen.

  • Strict Permission Triggers: You cannot ask for notification permissions as soon as the app loads. iOS will block it. The permission request (Notification.requestPermission()) must be attached to a direct user interaction, like tapping a button that says "Enable Notifications."

  • The Tech Stack: * Service Worker: You need a Javascript Service Worker running in the background of your PWA to catch the incoming push.

    • VAPID Keys: Your backend needs to generate a set of VAPID (Voluntary Application Server Identification) keys. These act as a cryptographic handshake so Apple's push servers know the notification is legitimately coming from your server.

Once the user is subscribed, your backend (acting as the suggestion engine) simply fires a Web Push payload to the endpoint generated by their device. The PWA wakes up, displays the notification natively on their lock screen, and tapping it opens your custom review flow.

2. Starting Playback from the PWA

If a user finishes their review, gets a suggestion for the next book in a series, and clicks "Listen," you have two ways to handle the handoff.

Route A: Deep Linking (The Smart/Easy Way) If you are treating your PWA purely as a "Companion App," you don't want to reinvent the wheel by building a custom streaming audio player. The official Audiobookshelf app (and third-party apps like Plappa) are already great at offline caching, CarPlay, and sleep timers.

  • How it works: When the user taps "Play," your PWA simply triggers a deep link/URL scheme pointing to that specific book's itemId on your ABS server. The OS intercepts the link, closes your PWA, and instantly snaps the user into the native ABS app with the book cued up.

Route B: Native API Web Streaming (The Heavy Lift) If you genuinely want them to start listening inside your PWA without bouncing to another app, you have to tap into the ABS streaming API. It works like this:

  1. Fetch Progress: You call /api/me/progress/{itemId} to see if they already have progress on this book, grabbing the lastPositionMs.

  2. Start a Session: You hit /api/session/play/{itemId}. ABS will generate a playback session and return a Sync Session ID and a Stream URL (usually an HLS .m3u8 playlist or a direct audio file).

  3. Play & Sync: You feed that stream URL into a web audio player (using a library like hls.js). Crucially, your PWA is now responsible for periodically sending PATCH requests to the ABS server with the Sync Session ID to update the user's timestamp. If you skip this, they will lose their place.

Given that your main pain point right now is just the clunky review and suggestion loop, Route A (Deep Linking back to their main player) will save you weeks of development time.

Does building a Service Worker for Web Push sound like something you are comfortable tackling, or does the backend notification management still feel like a hurdle?

conversation.question: You mentioned making a third party abs app like still. I do not want to pay for a developer license. I will be the only user in the app <!----><!---->
conversation.answer:
Building a PWA is the smartest move here. Since you are the only user and don't want to pay the $99/year Apple Developer fee, a native app is actually quite a headache to maintain.

Here is the breakdown of why the PWA wins for your specific "only-me" setup and how to handle the technical hurdles.

1. No Developer License? No Problem.

If you built a native app without a developer license, you would have to "sideload" it. This means connecting your phone to your computer every 7 days to re-sign the app, or it will stop opening.

  • PWA Advantage: Once you "Add to Home Screen," it stays there forever. No expiring certificates, no $99 fee, and no computer required for "refreshing" the app.

2. Handling Notifications (The Web Push Way)

Since it’s just for you, you don't need a complex notification service. You can use Web Push directly from your backend.

  • The Requirements: Your PWA must be served over HTTPS (use a free Cloudflare tunnel or Let's Encrypt) and have a Manifest file.

  • The Trigger: When you finish a book, your ABS server sends a Webhook to your custom PWA backend.

  • The Send: Your backend sends a standard Web Push to Apple's push servers. Your PWA's Service Worker will wake up, show the notification, and when you tap it, it will open the "Review" screen.

  • Verification: You'll use VAPID keys (free to generate) to prove to Apple that the notification is coming from your server.

3. "The Hand-off": Starting Playback

This is where it gets clever. You don't want to build a whole audio player in a PWA (it’s buggy on iOS). Instead, you want to Deep Link back to a real player.

Option A: Deep Linking to the Official ABS App

The official Audiobookshelf app supports custom URL schemes. In your PWA, when you show a suggested book, you can make the "Play" button a link like this: audiobookshelf://open?bookId=YOUR_BOOK_ID When you click it, iOS will instantly close your PWA and snap open the official ABS app directly to that book's page.

Option B: Deep Linking to Third-Party Players

If you prefer apps like Plappa or ShelfPlayer, they often have even better deep-linking support. You can usually find their specific URL schemes in their settings or documentation (e.g., plappa://book/ID).

Your Project Roadmap

  1. Backend: A simple Node.js or Python API that listens for the ABS "Finished Book" Webhook.

  2. Frontend: A single-page PWA (React, Vue, or even plain HTML/JS).

  3. The Hookup: * Open your PWA in Safari on your iPhone.

    • Share → Add to Home Screen.

    • Open the "App" and click a button to "Enable Notifications."

    • Save that subscription token to your database.

  4. The Flow: Finish book → ABS Webhook hits your API → API sends Web Push → You tap notification → Review book → Click "Next Book" → Deep Link opens the main player.

Does this "Companion App" approach feel like it covers the "clunky" part of the experience without adding the $99/year tax?

conversation.question: If i decided to use Plappa as the olayer what could i get it to do?  <!----><!----> <!----><br _ngcontent-ng-c2792978669="" class="ng-star-inserted"><!----> Syncing progress is notoriously bad for both abs and audible we need this for several features to work. <!----><!---->
conversation.answer:
You are hitting on the exact reason so many Audiobookshelf users end up frustrated with the official iOS app. Because the official ABS app is essentially a web app wrapped in a native shell (using Capacitor), iOS treats it like a background browser tab and aggressively kills its background processes. When that happens, your progress fails to sync back to the server, and your "Finished Book" webhooks never fire.

This is exactly where Plappa shines. Because it is built entirely in native Swift/SwiftUI, it has direct access to Apple's proper background audio and task-handling frameworks.

If you make Plappa your main player and use your PWA purely for the review/suggestion flow, here is what you gain:

1. Bulletproof Progress Syncing

This is the most important feature for your setup. Plappa communicates directly with the ABS API natively.

  • Reliable Webhooks: Because Plappa properly updates your server with your exact playback position (even when you pause from your lock screen or car), your server will actually know the precise moment you hit 100% completion. This means your "Finished Book" webhook will fire reliably, triggering your PWA notification.

  • Playback History: Recent updates to Plappa specifically overhauled how it syncs playback history with the ABS server, ensuring your timeline stays perfectly in sync across devices.

2. Deep Linking for Your PWA Handoff

Plappa registers a custom URL scheme in iOS (used for things like OAuth logins via plappa://oauth). This means in your custom PWA, when a user selects their next suggested book, you can create a link that immediately kicks them out of your PWA and snaps Plappa open, ready to play the next book. (Note: ShelfPlayer, another native iOS app, also explicitly supports Spotlight deep linking if you want to test both).

3. Native Apple Ecosystem Perks

Because it isn't a web wrapper, you get features the official app struggles with:

  • True Offline Support: You can download books, listen offline, and Plappa will securely cache your timestamps and sync them back to your ABS server the second you reconnect to Wi-Fi.

  • Apple Watch & CarPlay: Full native support for both, which is huge if you do a lot of listening on the go.

  • Smart Rewind: Automatically rewinds a few seconds if you pause the book for a long time, helping you catch your bearings.

The Verdict for Your Workflow

By offloading the actual playback to Plappa, you solve the sync problem completely. Plappa handles the audio and reliably tells the ABS server when a book is done. The ABS server fires a webhook to your backend, your backend pushes a notification to your PWA, and your PWA handles the custom review logic.

Since setting up the Web Push notification from your backend to your PWA will be the trickiest part of this new flow, would you like to map out how to generate the VAPID keys for Apple's push servers, or would you rather start by setting up the ABS webhook first?

conversation.question: One more question i am in the eu so apple allows some kind of sideloading and alternative store could that help us? Plappa is opensource so we could fork it <!----><!---->
conversation.answer:
That is a very clever thought process. Since Plappa is fully open source on GitHub (written in Swift/SwiftUI by LeoKlaus), you technically can download the code, tweak it, and compile it yourself.

However, the EU’s recent Digital Markets Act (DMA) changes will not save you from the Apple Developer fee if you want a permanent, hassle-free app.

Apple was forced to open the gates in the EU, but they did so with heavy restrictions that essentially lock out solo hobbyists trying to avoid the $99 fee. Here is the reality of the EU "sideloading" rules:

1. The EU Sideloading Catch: Notarization

Even in the EU, you cannot just download a raw .ipa file from a website and install it like an Android .apk. Whether you use an alternative app store (like AltStore PAL) or Apple's new "Web Distribution" (installing from a website), Apple still requires the app to be Notarized. To get an app notarized by Apple, you must have an active, paid $99/year Apple Developer account.

2. The "Web Distribution" Hurdle

If you thought about hosting the forked app on your own website using Apple's new EU Web Distribution rules, the requirements are genuinely absurd for a solo developer. To qualify, you must:

  • Pay the $99/year developer fee.

  • Be a member of the Developer Program for at least two continuous years.

  • Have an app that had over one million first annual installs in the EU in the prior year.

What happens if you fork Plappa anyway?

If you fork Plappa, add your custom review flow, and compile it in Xcode using a standard, free Apple ID, you fall back to Apple's classic global sideloading rules (using tools like Sideloadly or the regular AltStore).

If you go this route, you hit the 7-Day Limit: Because you don't have a paid developer license, the certificate signing your app expires every 7 days. You would have to connect your iPhone to your computer (or run a local server on your Wi-Fi) once a week to "refresh" the app. If you forget, your custom Plappa app will simply crash on launch until you re-sign it.

The Verdict

The EU laws were designed to help massive corporations like Epic Games and Spotify avoid Apple's 30% tax, not to help solo developers compile open-source apps for free.

If you don't want to pay the $99/year or deal with a weekly 7-day refresh chore, building a custom PWA and deep-linking into the official App Store version of Plappa is still the absolute best, most frictionless path forward.

Does the idea of setting up a lightweight PWA paired with official Plappa still sound like the winning combination, or are you tempted to brave the 7-day refresh cycle to have everything under one roof?

conversation.question: Okay how does testflight and sidestore work and can that help us? <!----><!---->
conversation.answer:
You are looking in exactly the right places. Both of these tools are designed to bypass the App Store, but they serve completely different purposes.

Here is the breakdown of how TestFlight and SideStore work, and whether they can finally give you the custom, free setup you are looking for.

1. TestFlight: The Official Route (That Still Costs Money)

TestFlight is Apple’s official beta testing platform. As a user, downloading apps from TestFlight is completely free and bypasses the App Store review process.

How it works: A developer uploads their app to Apple's servers, generates a link, and testers download the app. The app expires after 90 days, at which point the developer just uploads a new build. Can it help you? No. The catch is on the developer side. To be allowed to upload an app to TestFlight in the first place—even if it is strictly for internal testing and you are the only user—you must have an active, paid $99/year Apple Developer account. Since you want to avoid that fee, TestFlight is off the table.

2. SideStore: The Game Changer (Untethered Sideloading)

SideStore is a community-driven fork of the popular sideloading tool AltStore. This is the tool that actually can help you.

With standard sideloading on a free Apple ID, you have to plug your phone into a computer every 7 days to refresh the app certificates, or the apps crash. SideStore was built specifically to eliminate that chore.

How it works:

  1. The Setup: You only need a computer once to generate a pairing file and install the SideStore app onto your iPhone.

  2. The VPN Trick: SideStore installs a custom, local-only VPN on your iPhone (using a WireGuard loopback). It doesn't connect to an external server; it just reroutes traffic back into your phone.

  3. The Magic: When it is time to refresh your 7-day certificate, SideStore turns on this local VPN. The VPN intercepts Apple's verification checks and tricks your iPhone's operating system into thinking it is physically plugged into a Mac running Xcode.

  4. Auto-Refresh: SideStore automatically runs this fake handshake in the background via Wi-Fi while you sleep, resetting your 7-day timer indefinitely without you ever touching a computer.

The Catch with SideStore

Because SideStore still relies on Apple's free developer tier, you are still subject to Apple's hard limits for free accounts:

  • The 3-App Limit: Apple only allows a free Apple ID to have 3 sideloaded apps active at a time. Because SideStore counts as one of those apps, you effectively have 2 slots left for custom apps.

  • 10 App IDs Per Week: You can only install or test up to 10 unique apps every 7 days.

The Verdict: Can this help you?

Yes, absolutely. If you want everything natively under one roof and don't want to build a PWA, SideStore makes forking Plappa a highly viable option.

Your new workflow would look like this:

  1. You fork the open-source Plappa codebase on GitHub.

  2. You inject your custom Audiobookshelf logic (the review and suggestion flow) directly into the Swift code.

  3. You compile the app into an .ipa file using Xcode (for free).

  4. You install SideStore on your iPhone using your computer (just once).

  5. You send your custom Plappa .ipa file to your phone and install it using SideStore.

As long as you leave SideStore's background refresh turned on, your custom version of Plappa will live on your phone permanently, handling both audio playback and your review flow natively.

Does modifying Plappa's Swift codebase sound more appealing to you than building the web-based PWA companion app we discussed earlier?

conversation.question: My main concern with both are notifications i do not mind the pwa and seperate player but we need proper notifications <!----><!---->
conversation.answer:
You are absolutely right to make notifications your main priority. If the notification doesn't pop up the moment you finish the book, the entire "smooth experience" falls apart.

This requirement actually makes your decision much easier, because it reveals a massive hidden trap with sideloading.

The Sideloading Trap: Free Accounts = No Push Notifications

If you were leaning toward forking Plappa and using SideStore, you need to know this: Apple completely blocks remote push notifications for apps signed with a free Apple ID.

To send a push notification to an iOS app, the app must have an "entitlement" that connects it to the Apple Push Notification service (APNs). Apple locks the ability to generate APNs certificates strictly behind the $99/year paid developer account.

  • If you sideload a custom Plappa fork using SideStore, your ABS server will have no way to wake up the app remotely. Background push notifications simply will not work.

Why the PWA Wins the Notification Game

This is where the PWA (paired with the official Plappa app for playback) becomes your undeniable best option.

When you use Web Push notifications in a PWA, you aren't sending a push to a custom app; you are sending a push to iOS Safari, which already has all the proper, paid Apple APNs certificates built into the operating system.

  • You bypass the $99 fee entirely.

  • The notifications appear natively on your lock screen, just like any other app.

  • Tapping the notification immediately opens your PWA to the review screen.

The "Zero-Code" Notification Alternative

If setting up Service Workers and VAPID keys for PWA Web Push sounds a little too tedious for a one-person app, there is a bulletproof bridge you can use: ntfy or Pushover.

Instead of your PWA handling the notification, you let a dedicated app handle it, and use it as a portal to your PWA.

  1. The Setup: You install the free ntfy app from the iOS App Store.

  2. The Trigger: When you finish a book in Plappa, the ABS server fires a webhook to ntfy.

  3. The Payload: You format that webhook to include an "Action" URL.

  4. The Experience: Your phone buzzes with a native notification: "You finished Project Hail Mary! Tap to review." Tapping that notification instantly launches your PWA directly to the correct book's review page.

This gives you 100% reliable, instant notifications without having to code any complex push infrastructure or pay Apple a dime.

Would you prefer to build native Web Push directly into your PWA, or does using a simple bridge like ntfy to trigger the PWA sound like a faster win?

conversation.question: I like the pwa now. If we cannot make notifications work we have a backup plan.  <!----><!----> Can a pwa also work on a linux desktop? <!----><!---->
conversation.answer:
Yes, absolutely. One of the greatest strengths of a Progressive Web App is that it is completely platform-agnostic.

If you build your PWA to solve the iOS issue, it will work flawlessly on your Linux desktop with exactly zero extra code.

Here is how the PWA experience translates to a Linux desktop environment (like GNOME, KDE Plasma, or Cinnamon):

1. Installation & Desktop Integration

You install the PWA on Linux exactly the same way you do on a phone.

  • Chromium Browsers (Chrome, Brave, Edge, Vivaldi): These have flawless, native PWA support on Linux. You just open your PWA url, click the "Install" icon in the address bar, and you are done.

  • The Result: The browser creates a native .desktop file for your PWA. It will show up in your Linux application launcher just like a native app (like VLC or LibreOffice). When you click it, it opens in its own clean, standalone window without browser tabs or an address bar.

  • Firefox Users: Firefox historically removed native PWA support, but as of early 2026, Mozilla is actively building it back into the browser under a feature called "Taskbar Tabs." Until that fully lands on Linux, you can get the exact same native experience using the highly popular PWAsForFirefox extension.

2. Desktop Notifications

Remember how Web Push bypasses Apple's restrictions on iOS? It works identically on Linux. When your backend fires a Web Push notification to your PWA, the Linux browser intercepts it and hands it off to your desktop environment's native notification daemon (e.g., notify-osd on GNOME or dunst).

You will get a standard desktop pop-up in the corner of your screen saying "You finished Project Hail Mary! Click to review." Clicking that desktop notification will instantly open the PWA window.

3. The Unified "Only-Me" Ecosystem

Because it's a PWA, your service worker and VAPID keys don't care what device you are on. If you install the PWA on both your iPhone and your Linux desktop, they will generate separate subscription tokens. When your ABS server fires the "Finished Book" webhook, your backend can just loop through your tokens and push the notification to both devices simultaneously.

Whether you are walking with your iPhone or sitting at your Linux PC, you will get the instant prompt to review your book and see the next suggestion.

It sounds like the PWA path fixes both your mobile and desktop workflows in one swing. Are you planning to build the PWA backend in Node.js, Python, or something else you are already running on your Linux machine?

conversation.question: I have different use cases on phone and desktop.  <!----><!----> I only listen on my phone.  <!----><!----> On desktop i can mange the service and monitor what is happening. Add new books and tune settings.  <!----><!----> <!----><br _ngcontent-ng-c2792978669="" class="ng-star-inserted"><!----> I would like to be able to pick what to hear next on my desktop and have it reflected on my phone so pressing play is seemless.  <!----><!----> <!----><br _ngcontent-ng-c2792978669="" class="ng-star-inserted"><!----> Also we could make a queue of what to hear next so windlass plays the next book automatically when a book is done <!----><!---->
conversation.answer:
This sounds like the perfect division of labor: your Linux desktop acts as mission control, and your phone acts as a pure, frictionless listening device.

(Side note: I am assuming "windlass" was a wild swipe-typing autocorrect for "Plappa" or "the app," unless there is a secret new audiobook player named Windlass that I haven't heard of!)

Building a queue system to bridge your desktop and phone is actually very straightforward with the PWA and backend setup we have been discussing. Because Audiobookshelf doesn't have a highly robust "auto-play next book" queue built into its native architecture, your custom backend can handle the heavy lifting.

Here is exactly how you can build this "Mission Control" queue system so it flows seamlessly to your phone:

1. The Desktop "Mission Control" (PWA)

Since your PWA will run beautifully as a standalone app on your Linux desktop, you can build a dedicated "Queue Manager" tab into it.

  • The Interface: Your PWA fetches your library via the ABS API (/api/libraries/{id}/items). You can browse your books and click an "Add to Queue" button.

  • The Brains: Your backend (Node.js/Python) stores this queue list in a simple lightweight database (like SQLite or even a JSON file) ordered 1, 2, 3.

2. Making the Next Book "Seamless" on Phone

There are two ways to execute the handoff when you finish a book, depending on how automated you want it to be.

Option A: The "One-Tap" Deep Link (Highly Reliable) Audiobook apps like Plappa are designed around single-book playback, so forcing them to automatically load and play a new book entirely in the background without user input is heavily restricted by iOS. The smoothest, most reliable Apple-friendly way is the one-tap notification:

  1. You finish Book 1. Plappa natively syncs the 100% progress to your ABS server.

  2. ABS fires the Webhook to your backend.

  3. Your backend checks your custom Queue, sees Book 2 is up next, and pops it off the list.

  4. The backend sends a Web Push to your phone: "Book Finished! Up Next: Project Hail Mary. Tap to play."

  5. You tap the notification, it triggers the deep link (plappa://book/ID), and Plappa instantly opens with Book 2 on the screen, ready for you to just hit play.

Option B: The Native ABS "Playlist" Route If you truly want the next book to start playing automatically without even touching your phone, you have to use Audiobookshelf's native Playlists feature.

  1. On your desktop, instead of building a custom queue database, your PWA uses the ABS API to add books to an official ABS Playlist.

  2. On your phone, you open Plappa (or the official ABS app) and initiate playback from the Playlist rather than the individual book.

  3. The Catch: Not all third-party iOS apps support continuous auto-play for ABS playlists yet. The official ABS app handles it better, but as we discussed, it struggles with background syncing. You would need to test if Plappa continuously rolls over to the next item in a playlist flawlessly on iOS.

The Verdict for Your Flow

Using your PWA backend to manage a custom queue (Option A) gives you absolute control. You can sit at your Linux desktop, drag and drop the next three books you want to listen to, and know that your phone will seamlessly serve up the right deep link the moment you finish your current listen.

Since we are leaning into this custom backend doing a lot of the heavy lifting for the queue and notifications, are you comfortable writing a small SQLite or JSON database to hold your "Up Next" list, or would you prefer to just use ABS's built-in tagging system to mark books as "queued"?

conversation.question: Lets invistigate the playlist a bit. Could we modify the playlist continuously so the next book just plays?  <!----><!----> I like the seemlessness of the next book just starting on its own if i am listening but use my hand for something less where i cannot just operate my phone eg painting <!----><!---->
conversation.answer:
This is a fantastic use case. When you are covered in paint, the last thing you want to do is wash your hands, unlock your phone, open a notification, and press play just to keep the story going.

The short answer is yes, this is completely possible. The "auto-play next" functionality is exactly what Playlists were built for, and the ecosystem finally supports it.

Here is how you can achieve that truly hands-free, continuous playback using Playlists and Plappa.

1. Plappa Now Supports Continuous Playback

In its recent updates, Plappa specifically added two major features: Playlist Support and an "Up Next" Queue.

If you initiate playback from a Playlist rather than an individual book, Plappa acts like a standard music player (like Spotify). When Book A hits 100%, it will automatically buffer and start playing Book B without a single moment of silence or user input.

2. Modifying Playlists via Your PWA/Mission Control

Audiobookshelf's API fully supports creating and editing playlists remotely. From your Linux desktop PWA, you can use the ABS REST API to modify a dedicated playlist (let's call it the "Active Queue" playlist).

  • The "Ahead-of-Time" Approach (Recommended): While sitting at your desktop, you select the 2 or 3 books you want to listen to while painting and push them to the "Active Queue" playlist. When you start painting, you just hit play on that playlist in Plappa. It will seamlessly roll through them.

  • The "Just-in-Time" Approach (Tricky): You asked about modifying the playlist continuously. You could technically have your backend add "Book 2" to the playlist at the exact moment "Book 1" hits 99%. However, mobile apps generally cache the playlist data when you first hit "Play." If you alter the playlist on the server mid-stream, Plappa might not realize a new book was added until you refresh the app. Pre-loading the playlist queue from your desktop is much safer.

3. The New Workflow

If you want to build this into your desktop "Mission Control" PWA, the architecture looks like this:

  1. On Linux (The PWA): You build a visual queue in your PWA. When you drag a book into the "Next Up" slot, your backend sends an API request (POST /api/libraries/{id}/playlists) to update your "Active Queue" playlist on the ABS server.

  2. On iPhone (Plappa): You open Plappa, go to your Playlists tab, and hit Play on the "Active Queue."

  3. The Hands-Free Experience: You start painting. When the first book ends, Plappa's native continuous playback kicks in, and the next book starts immediately.

  4. The Cleanup: When you finish painting, your ABS webhooks can trigger your backend to automatically remove the finished books from the playlist, keeping it clean for your next session.

This gives you the best of both worlds: the strict organization and custom logic on your Linux machine, and a completely hands-free, native Apple audio experience while you work.
